video_title: Gold Falls for the Third Session, Oil Edges Higher | Horizons Middle East & Africa 7/01/2026
gist: Bloomberg's Horizons Middle East & Africa opens H2 2026 with markets digesting a hawkish incoming Fed governor (Kevin Warsh): gold falls a third session below its 200-day average, the yen drifts to a four-decade low near 163 as Japan weighs intervention, and oil trades near $70-73 with banks (Goldman, Morgan Stanley) flagging a return to oversupply as Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizes. Guests debate AI/semiconductor sustainability, copper tariff positioning, central-bank gold demand, Ethiopia's debt restructuring, and Dubai's ultra-tight office market.

themes.theme: Hawkish Fed pivot under Warsh reprices risk assets

insights.claim: An incoming Fed governor (Kevin Warsh) is delivering hawkish statements to establish credibility and calm bond markets, driving expectations of possible rate hikes rather than cuts and lifting the dollar.
insights.speaker: Rahul
insights.so_what: A hawkish Fed regime shift is the master driver behind the dollar, gold, bitcoin and yen moves discussed all show.
insights.quote: THE INCOMING FED GOVERNOR WANTED TO ESTABLISH CREDIBILITY, WHICH IS WHY YOU HAD A LOT OF STATEMENTS INTERPRETED AS HAWKISH.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has fallen below $60,000 to a 21-month low, dropping under its 200-week moving average, driven by fear of Fed rate hikes after recently trading above $100,000.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: Crypto is acting as a high-beta casualty of the hawkish rate repricing, a tape-read for risk appetite.
insights.quote: IT IS NOT A 21 MONTH LOW, BELOW 60,000 DOLLARS... IT HAS FALLEN BELOW THE 200 WEEK MOVING AVERAGE

insights.claim: Warsh's speech in Europe and Thursday's June nonfarm payrolls report are the key near-term catalysts markets are watching for the interest-rate path.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: Positioning ahead of payrolls and Warsh's rhetoric frames the intervention, gold and dollar bets across the session.

themes.theme: Yen at four-decade low and the intervention watch

insights.claim: The yen is drifting weaker at a snail's pace toward 163-164 per dollar precisely because the market fears further Japanese intervention, keeping moves gradual rather than sharp.
insights.speaker: Paul
insights.so_what: Intervention risk itself is dampening yen volatility; a fast break would signal Tokyo has lost control.
insights.quote: THE MARKET IS NERVOUS ABOUT THE POSSIBILITY THAT THERE WILL BE FURTHER INTERVENTION... THE MOVE IS A SNAIL'S PACE FOR THE YEN.

insights.claim: Japan may wait to intervene until after Thursday's US nonfarm payrolls and act on the Friday US holiday, when thin liquidity lets them get more impact for their money.
insights.speaker: Paul
insights.so_what: Timing edge: a Friday-holiday yen intervention is the tactical scenario FX traders should hedge for.
insights.quote: PARTICULARLY ON THE FRIDAY WHEN IT IS A U.S. HOLIDAY AND THEY COULD GET MORE BANG FOR THEIR BACK.

insights.claim: Japan has not signaled intervention as strongly as last time, so watch policymaker rhetoric for a clear indication they've had enough; longer-term it will take more than intervention to turn the yen around for good.
insights.speaker: Paul
insights.so_what: Rhetoric, not price alone, is the leading indicator of imminent FX action.
insights.quote: LONGER-TERM, IT WILL TAKE MORE THAN THAT [TO] TURN IT AROUND FOR GOOD.

themes.theme: Oil: glut returns as Hormuz normalizes and OPEC unshackles

insights.claim: Oil posted its biggest quarterly drop since the pandemic as traders bet on a returning glut, with Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizing after wartime disruption.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: The war premium is unwinding fast; the base case is reverting to a fundamentally oversupplied market.
insights.quote: OIL POSTED ITS BIGGEST QUARTERLY DROP SINCE THE [PANDEMIC] AS TRADERS BE[T] GLUT [RE]TURN WITH HORMUZ TRAFFIC NORMALIZING.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley cut its 2026 average Brent estimate to about $75 a barrel, lowering Q3 by $15 and Q4 by $5, while Goldman Sachs warns of oversupply later this year.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: Sell-side is actively marking down oil forecasts, reinforcing the bearish supply thesis.
insights.quote: MORGAN STANLEY YESTERDAY BROUGHT DOWN THEIR AVERAGE PRICE ESTIMATE FOR BRENT... $15 LOWER FOR THE THIRD QUARTER. $5 LOWER FOR THE FOURTH QUARTER. ABOUT $75 A BARREL

insights.claim: The 2026 oil glut is driven by both a supply wave (waiting/loaded tankers returning, OPEC lifting production now that it's unconstrained by quotas below capacity) and a demand hit (China drawing down stockpiles and cutting refining, Indian chemical producers halting purchases).
insights.speaker: Anthony DePaulo
insights.so_what: The bearish oil setup is structural on both sides of the balance, not just a Hormuz relief trade.
insights.quote: A WAVE OF SUPPLY IS COMING IN, PARTICULARLY AS TANKERS THAT WERE WAITING AND LOADED ARE BACK ON THE MARKET.

insights.claim: The UAE's biggest oil company (ADNOC) is switching pricing of its offshore crude from an Abu Dhabi exchange-derived Murban benchmark to the Dubai benchmark, because market upheaval strained the old linkage and made barrels hard to price.
insights.speaker: Anthony DePaulo
insights.so_what: A benchmark switch signals how badly wartime dislocations broke Gulf crude price discovery.

insights.claim: ADNOC is in line to buy Shell's South African retail fuel stations, an asset valued around $1 billion with hundreds of stations, as Gulf producers seek downstream outlets in Africa.
insights.speaker: Anthony DePaulo
insights.so_what: Gulf NOCs are buying retail distribution to secure ready buyers for their crude in African markets.
insights.quote: IT LOOKS LIKE ADNOC IS IN LINE FOR THAT. THIS ASSET IS PROBABLY A BILLION DOLLARS VALUATION WITH HUNDREDS OF STATIONS ACROSS SOUTH AFRICA.

insights.claim: Gold has slipped a third day after a technical breakdown below its 200-day moving average, pressured by Warsh's hawkish tone and US inflation concerns, but is expected to recover over coming months as Fed hikes stay costly and hard to deliver.
insights.speaker: George
insights.so_what: Near-term technical weakness is seen as a dip within an intact bull cycle, not a top.
insights.quote: TOWARDS THE END OF THE YEAR WE STILL SEE A [HUGE] AMOUNT OF INTEREST, PARTICULARLY FROM CENTRAL BANKS IN BUYING GOLD.

insights.claim: A survey out yesterday showed 45% of central banks intend to increase gold holdings over the next 12 months, and gold ETF holdings (~10 million ounces two months ago) have not seen real selling, establishing a rising price floor each cycle.
insights.speaker: George
insights.so_what: Persistent central-bank and Asian demand underpins a structurally higher gold floor despite the selloff.
insights.quote: THE LATEST SURVEY... SHOWED A HIGH NUMBER, 45% SAYING THEY INTENDED TO INCREASE HOLDING[S]

insights.claim: Copper is trading a very short-term tariff story: LME/exchange stocks have risen ~800,000 tons over four months as metal was dragged to the US ahead of a possible 10-15% tariff, so investors should be wary that supply is recovering and inventory is building.
insights.speaker: George
insights.so_what: The bullish copper-demand narrative masks a tariff-driven inventory build that could unwind sharply.
insights.quote: WHILE PEOPLE SAY COPPER DEMAND IS STRONG WE HAVE SEEN A BUILD-UP OF INVENTORY OVER THE PAST FOUR MONTHS.

insights.claim: China's rare-earth dominance gives it strong short-term geopolitical leverage, but this is a processing problem not an availability problem, and the West can solve it in a year or two by building processing facilities.
insights.speaker: George
insights.so_what: Rare-earth leverage is a transient bottleneck, not a permanent structural chokehold on the West.
insights.quote: THIS IS A PROCESSING PROBLEM, NOT AVAILABILITY AT ALL.

insights.claim: Alcoa agreed to buy Alumina in a $5.6 billion deal covering assets in Australia, Brazil and South Africa, saying it strengthens its position as aluminum demand grows.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: M&A signals producer confidence in structural aluminum demand growth.

themes.theme: AI/semiconductor rally: due for a breather

insights.claim: Asian equities booked their strongest quarter in ~17 years and US equities their best in six years, both led by a semiconductor/AI rally in memory names.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: The AI-memory trade is the single dominant driver of global equity performance into H2.

insights.claim: The AI trade should take a breather: memory leaders like Micron, SK Hynix and Samsung look optically cheap because earnings jumped 10x, but new supply is coming and the next few months will test ROI as Mag 7 has underperformed.
insights.speaker: Rahul
insights.so_what: Cheap-looking semis may be a peak-earnings trap once supply floods in and AI ROI is questioned.
insights.quote: OPTICALLY THEY LOOK CHEAP ON EARNINGS, BUT THE EARNINGS HAVE GONE UP 10 TIMES AND WHEN THE SUPPLY COMES IN

insights.claim: Watch Microsoft closely for AI capex direction; the base case is maintained spending, and any cut would hurt the AI trade, with hyperscalers pushing private clouds and warning against giving all data to Anthropic.
insights.speaker: Rahul
insights.so_what: Microsoft capex guidance is the swing variable for the entire AI infrastructure trade.
insights.quote: WATCH MICROSOFT FOR CAPEX BUT THE BASE CASE FOR THE MARKET IS... WHERE WE MAINTAIN THE MOVE.

insights.claim: Mag 7 AI-infrastructure bond issuance could approach $1 trillion by year-end and exceed $1.3 trillion next year, testing bond-market appetite for $300-400 billion of paper from an unlevered set of companies while token spend has plateaued since May.
insights.speaker: Rahul
insights.so_what: Massive AI debt supply plus plateauing token demand is a flashing risk for the funding side of the boom.
insights.quote: THEY HAVE BEEN CLOSE TO ONE TRILLION BY THE END OF THIS YEAR. NEXT YEAR... ABOVE ONE POINT 3 TRILLION

insights.claim: Despite talk of a bubble, this is not 2000 or 2007; AI is real in parts of the market, so expect 20-30% pullbacks and a time correction rather than a 70% collapse.
insights.speaker: Rahul
insights.so_what: Frames AI drawdown risk as a correction to buy, not a dot-com-style structural bust.
insights.quote: YOU AREN'T SITTING ON WHAT WE HAD IN THE PREVIOUS CYCLE, 2000 OR 2007... I WOULD NOT SAY THERE WOULD BE 70%... BUT 20% PULLBACKS

insights.claim: The US lifted foreign-access restrictions on Anthropic's Fable Five model (a less powerful version of Mythos Five) after Anthropic adjusted the models to address national-security concerns, clearing wider global public use.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low
insights.so_what: US export policy on frontier AI models is loosening for vetted use, easing a geopolitical overhang.

themes.theme: US-Iran diplomacy and Middle East geopolitics

insights.claim: US negotiators Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff held talks with regional leaders in Doha on an Iran deal, and President Trump ruled out military intervention for now in favor of the diplomatic route.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: A diplomatic-over-military posture is the key catalyst deflating the oil war premium.
insights.quote: PRESIDENT TRUMP SAID NO MILITARY INTERVENTION FOR NOW. HE IS DECIDING TO GO DOWN THE DIPLOMATIC ROUTE.

insights.claim: That the US keeps calling US-Iran talks 'positive' despite vociferous Iranian remarks and extreme action over the weekend signals how much Washington is prepared to tolerate to keep negotiations alive.
insights.speaker: Stuart Livingston Wallace
insights.so_what: US tolerance for provocation suggests strong commitment to a deal, reducing near-term escalation odds.
insights.quote: THAT YOU CONTINUE TO SAY IT IS POSITIVE I THINK SHOWS THE ATTITUDE THAT THE U.S. IS GOING INTO THESE NEGOTIATIONS WITH.

insights.claim: Strait of Hormuz traffic is resuming but remains patchy with ships switching signals on and off, so it is premature to bake normalized global supply into certainty given sporadic fire in recent weeks.
insights.speaker: Stuart Livingston Wallace
insights.so_what: The market may be pricing Hormuz relief too confidently; shippers remain reluctant to treat it as business as usual.
insights.quote: THE SHIPPING INDUSTRY WILL BE RELUCTANT TO THINK IT IS BUSINESS AS NORMAL WHEN YOU SAW WHAT TRANSPIRED ACROSS THE STRAIT.

themes.theme: Africa debt and Gulf real estate

insights.claim: Ethiopia reached an in-principle restructuring deal with ~45% of bondholders, exchanging its $1 billion eurobond for an $880 million bond maturing in 2029 plus a warrant to subscribe to a future issue, with IMF and official-creditor backing, averting a lengthy legal battle.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Zabasajja
insights.so_what: A high-recovery, frontloaded-cashflow outcome could revive Ethiopia's access to international capital markets.
insights.quote: CREDITORS WILL RECEIVE $880 MILLION MATURING IN 20[29]... THE DEAL DOES HAVE IMF AND OFFICIAL CREDITOR BACKING.

insights.claim: A Jefferies strategist sees scope for Ethiopia's bond to extend gains, viewing the agreement as confirming a high-recovery outcome with limited debt relief required and strong frontloaded cash flows.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Zabasajja
insights.so_what: Sell-side sees the restructuring as creditor-friendly, supporting further bond upside.

insights.claim: Dubai's prime office market is extremely tight: three new buildings went design-to-completion in a record ~24 months yet were fully pre-leased before handover, prime vacancy near 0.7%, and rents up 17% year-on-year, giving landlords strong pricing power.
insights.speaker: Middle East Finance Reporter
insights.so_what: Sticky business demand makes Dubai office property a steady cash source and a rare landlord's market despite regional war risk.
insights.quote: RENTS HAVE GONE UP 17% FROM THE FIRST QUARTER OF LAST YEAR AND PRIME REAL ESTATE... IS ZERO .7%

insights.claim: Emirates NBD is in talks to buy HSBC's Turkey unit though a deal may not be reached, part of a push to expand Turkish operations after a 2024 CEO appointment.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar
insights.so_what: Gulf banks are eyeing Turkish M&A expansion, a watch item for regional banking consolidation.
